title: Equator

infobox.value: is the [circle of latitude](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude") that divides [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") into the [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") [Hemispheres](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_Earth "Hemispheres of Earth"). It is an [imaginary line](/wiki/Imaginary_line "Imaginary line") located at 0 degrees [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), about 21,639 nautical miles (40,075 kilometres; 24,902 miles) in circumference, halfway between the [North](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") and [South](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") Poles.[[1]](#cite_note-National-1) The term can also be used for any other celestial body that is roughly spherical.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Imaginary line halfway between Earth's North and South poles

This article is about Earth's equator. For its projection in the sky, see [Celestial equator](/wiki/Celestial_equator "Celestial equator"). For other uses, see [Equator (disambiguation)](/wiki/Equator_(disambiguation) "Equator (disambiguation)").

For the country, see [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador").

[![Line across the Earth](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/World_location_map_%28equirectangular_180%29.svg/330px-World_location_map_%28equirectangular_180%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_location_map_(equirectangular_180).svg "Line across the Earth")

0°

The equator on a [map of Earth](/wiki/World_map "World map") (interactive map)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0b/Solstice_revolving_earth.gif)](/wiki/File:Solstice_revolving_earth.gif)

The equator (yellow line) at the December solstice. The direction of the Sun is to the left.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Equator_and_Prime_Meridian.svg/500px-Equator_and_Prime_Meridian.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Equator_and_Prime_Meridian.svg)

Countries and territories that are intersected by the equator (red) or the [Prime Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian") (blue), which intersect at "[Null Island](/wiki/Null_Island "Null Island")".

The **equator** is the [circle of latitude](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude") that divides [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") into the [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") [Hemispheres](/wiki/Hemispheres_of_Earth "Hemispheres of Earth"). It is an [imaginary line](/wiki/Imaginary_line "Imaginary line") located at 0 degrees [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), about 21,639 nautical miles (40,075 kilometres; 24,902 miles) in circumference, halfway between the [North](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") and [South](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") Poles.[[1]](#cite_note-National-1) The term can also be used for any other celestial body that is roughly spherical.

In [spatial](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space") (3D) [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry"), as applied in [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), the equator of a rotating [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid") (such as a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet")) is the parallel (circle of latitude) at which latitude is defined to be 0°. It is an imaginary line on the spheroid, equidistant from its [poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole"), dividing it into northern and southern hemispheres. In other words, it is the intersection of the spheroid with the [plane](/wiki/Plane_(geometry) "Plane (geometry)") [perpendicular](/wiki/Perpendicular "Perpendicular") to its axis of [rotation](/wiki/Rotation "Rotation") and midway between its [geographical poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole").

On and near the equator (on Earth), noontime [sunlight](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") appears almost directly overhead (no more than about 23° from the [zenith](/wiki/Zenith "Zenith")) every day, year-round. Consequently, the equator has a rather stable daytime temperature throughout the year. On the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") (approximately 20 March and 23 September) the [subsolar point](/wiki/Subsolar_point "Subsolar point") crosses Earth's equator at a shallow angle, sunlight shines perpendicular to Earth's axis of rotation, and all latitudes have nearly a 12-hour day and 12-hour night.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name is derived from [medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") word *aequator*, in the phrase *circulus aequator diei et noctis*, meaning 'circle equalizing day and night', from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *aequare* 'make equal'.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c9/Equator_monument.jpg/250px-Equator_monument.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equator_monument.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Equator_sign_kenya.jpg/250px-Equator_sign_kenya.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equator_sign_kenya.jpg)

*Left*: A monument marking the equator near the city of [Pontianak, Indonesia](/wiki/Pontianak,_Indonesia "Pontianak, Indonesia")  
*Right*: Road sign marking the equator near [Nanyuki](/wiki/Nanyuki "Nanyuki"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya")

The [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") of the Earth's equator is, by definition, 0° (zero [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)")) of arc. The equator is one of the five notable [circles of latitude](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude") on Earth; the other four are the two [polar circles](/wiki/Polar_circle "Polar circle") (the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle") and the [Antarctic Circle](/wiki/Antarctic_Circle "Antarctic Circle")) and the two [tropical circles](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics") (the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") and the [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn")). The equator is the only line of latitude which is also a [great circle](/wiki/Great_circle "Great circle")—meaning, one whose [plane](/wiki/Plane_(mathematics) "Plane (mathematics)") passes through the center of the globe. The plane of Earth's equator, when projected outwards to the [celestial sphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere"), defines the [celestial equator](/wiki/Celestial_equator "Celestial equator").

In the cycle of Earth's [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season"), the equatorial plane runs through the Sun twice a [year](/wiki/Year "Year"): on the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") in [March](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox") and [September](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox"). To a person on Earth, the Sun [appears to travel along](/wiki/Subsolar_point "Subsolar point") the equator (or along the celestial equator) at these times.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Equator_Sao_Tome.jpg/250px-Equator_Sao_Tome.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equator_Sao_Tome.jpg)

The equator marked as it crosses [Ilhéu das Rolas](/wiki/Ilh%C3%A9u_das_Rolas "Ilhéu das Rolas"), in [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Equator_Line_Monument%2C_Macap%C3%A1_city%2C_Brazil.jpg/250px-Equator_Line_Monument%2C_Macap%C3%A1_city%2C_Brazil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equator_Line_Monument,_Macap%C3%A1_city,_Brazil.jpg)

The Marco Zero monument marking the equator in [Macapá](/wiki/Macap%C3%A1 "Macapá"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")

Locations on the equator experience the shortest [sunrises](/wiki/Sunrise "Sunrise") and [sunsets](/wiki/Sunset "Sunset") because the Sun's [daily path](/wiki/Sun_path "Sun path") is nearly perpendicular to the [horizon](/wiki/Horizon "Horizon") for most of the year. The length of [daylight](/wiki/Daylight "Daylight") (sunrise to sunset) is almost constant throughout the year; it is about 14 minutes longer than nighttime due to [atmospheric refraction](/wiki/Atmospheric_refraction "Atmospheric refraction") and the fact that sunrise begins (or sunset ends) as the upper limb, not the center, of the Sun's disk contacts the horizon.

Earth [bulges slightly](/wiki/Equatorial_bulge "Equatorial bulge") at the equator; its average diameter is 12,742 km (7,918 mi), but the diameter at the equator is about 43 km (27 mi) greater than at the poles.[[1]](#cite_note-National-1)

Sites near the equator, such as the [Guiana Space Centre](/wiki/Guiana_Space_Centre "Guiana Space Centre") in [Kourou](/wiki/Kourou "Kourou"), French Guiana, are good locations for [spaceports](/wiki/Spaceport "Spaceport") as they have the fastest [rotational speed](/wiki/Rotational_speed "Rotational speed") of any latitude, 460 metres (1,510 ft)/sec. The added [velocity](/wiki/Delta-v "Delta-v") reduces the fuel needed to launch spacecraft eastward (in the direction of Earth's rotation) to orbit, while simultaneously avoiding costly maneuvers to flatten [inclination](/wiki/Inclination "Inclination") during missions such as the [Apollo Moon landings](/wiki/Apollo_Program "Apollo Program").[[4]](#cite_note-Moonport-4)

## Geodesy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geodesy")]

Further information: [Earth ellipsoid](/wiki/Earth_ellipsoid "Earth ellipsoid") and [Reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid")

### Precise location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Precise location")]

The precise location of the equator is not truly fixed; the true equatorial plane is perpendicular to the [Earth's rotation axis](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation_axis "Earth's rotation axis"), which [drifts](/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion") about 9 metres (30 ft) during a year.

Geological samples show that the equator significantly changed positions between 48 and 12 million years ago, as sediment deposited by ocean thermal currents at the equator shifted. The deposits by thermal currents are determined by the axis of Earth, which determines solar coverage of [Earth's surface](/wiki/Earth#Surface "Earth"). Changes in Earth's axis can also be observed in the geographical layout of volcanic island chains, which are created by shifting hot spots under Earth's crust as the axis and crust move.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This is consistent with the [Indian tectonic plate](/wiki/Indian_Plate "Indian Plate") colliding with the [Eurasian tectonic plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate"), which is causing the [Himalayan](/wiki/Himalaya "Himalaya") uplift.

### Exact length

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Exact length")]

Main article: [Earth's circumference](/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference "Earth's circumference")

The International Association of Geodesy (IAG) and the International Astronomical Union (IAU) use an equatorial radius of 6,378.1366 kilometres (3,963.1903 mi) (codified as the IAU 2009 value).[[6]](#cite_note-6) This equatorial radius is also in the 2003 and 2010 IERS Conventions.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is also the equatorial radius used for the IERS 2003 ellipsoid. If it were really circular, the length of the equator would then be exactly 2π times the radius, namely 40,075.0142 kilometres (24,901.4594 mi). The [GRS 80](/wiki/GRS_80 "GRS 80") (Geodetic Reference System 1980) as approved and adopted by the IUGG at its Canberra, Australia meeting of 1979 has an equatorial radius of 6,378.137 kilometres (3,963.191 mi). The [WGS 84](/wiki/WGS_84 "WGS 84") (World Geodetic System 1984) which is a standard for use in cartography, geodesy, and satellite navigation including [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System"), also has an equatorial radius of 6,378.137 kilometres (3,963.191 mi). For both GRS 80 and WGS 84, this results in a length for the equator of 40,075.0167 kilometres (24,901.4609 mi).

The [geographical mile](/wiki/Geographical_mile "Geographical mile") is defined as one [arc-minute](/wiki/Arc-minute "Arc-minute") of the equator, so it has different values depending on which radius is assumed. For example, by WSG-84, the distance is 1,855.3248 metres (6,087.024 ft), while by IAU-2000, it is 1,855.3257 metres (6,087.027 ft). This is a difference of less than one millimetre (0.039 in) over the total distance (approximately 1.86 kilometres or 1.16 miles).

Earth is commonly modeled as a [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") flattened 0.336% along its axis. This makes the equator 0.16% longer than a [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") (a great circle passing through the two poles). The IUGG standard meridian is, to the nearest millimetre, 40,007.862917 kilometres (24,859.733480 mi), one arc-minute of which is 1,852.216 metres (6,076.82 ft), explaining the [SI](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") standardization of the [nautical mile](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") as 1,852 metres (6,076 ft), more than 3 metres (9.8 ft) less than the [geographical mile](/wiki/Geographical_mile "Geographical mile").

The [sea-level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") surface of Earth (the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid")) is irregular, so the actual length of the equator is not so easy to determine. *[Aviation Week and Space Technology](/wiki/Aviation_week_and_space_technology "Aviation week and space technology")* on 9 October 1961 reported that measurements using the [Transit](/wiki/Transit_(satellite) "Transit (satellite)") IV-A satellite had shown the equatorial diameter from longitude 11° West to 169° East to be 300 metres (1,000 ft) greater than its diameter ninety degrees away.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The equator passes over approximately 8714 km of land (21.7%) and 31,361 km of sea (78.3%).[[10]](#cite_note-10) It passes through the land of eleven [sovereign states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"). [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") is the country straddling the greatest length of the equatorial line across both land and sea. The equator also passes through the [territorial seas](/wiki/Territorial_waters#Territorial_sea "Territorial waters") of three countries: [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") (south of [Gaafu Dhaalu Atoll](/wiki/Gaafu_Dhaalu_Atoll "Gaafu Dhaalu Atoll")), [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") (south of [Buariki Island](/wiki/Buariki_(Aranuka) "Buariki (Aranuka)")), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (south of [Baker Island](/wiki/Baker_Island "Baker Island")).

Despite its name, no part of [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea") lies on the equator. However, its island of [Annobón](/wiki/Annob%C3%B3n "Annobón") is 155 km (96 mi) south of the equator, and the rest of the country lies to the north. [France](/wiki/France "France") ([Mayotte](/wiki/Mayotte "Mayotte"), [Réunion](/wiki/R%C3%A9union "Réunion")), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") ([Bouvet Island](/wiki/Bouvet_Island "Bouvet Island")), and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") ([British Antarctic Territory](/wiki/British_Antarctic_Territory "British Antarctic Territory"), [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory"), [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands"), [Pitcairn Islands](/wiki/Pitcairn_Islands "Pitcairn Islands"), [Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha](/wiki/Saint_Helena,_Ascension_and_Tristan_da_Cunha "Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha"), [South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands")) are the other three [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere")-based countries which have territories in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere").

## Equatorial seasons and climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Equatorial seasons and climate")]

Main articles: [Season](/wiki/Season "Season") and [Effect of Sun angle on climate](/wiki/Effect_of_Sun_angle_on_climate "Effect of Sun angle on climate")

Further information: [Tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Seasons.svg/330px-Seasons.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Seasons.svg)

Diagram of the seasons, showing the situation at the December solstice. Regardless of the time of day (i.e. [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s rotation on its axis), the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") will be dark, and the [South Pole](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") will be illuminated; see also [polar night](/wiki/Polar_night "Polar night") and [polar day](/wiki/Polar_day "Polar day"). In addition to the density of [incident](/wiki/Angle_of_incidence_(optics) "Angle of incidence (optics)") light, the [dissipation](/wiki/Dissipation "Dissipation") of light in the [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Earth "Atmosphere of Earth") is greater when it falls at a shallow angle. Note that the equator is not under the [subsolar point](/wiki/Subsolar_point "Subsolar point") during this time of year.

Seasons result from the tilt of Earth's axis away from a line perpendicular to the plane of its revolution around the Sun. Throughout the year, the Northern and Southern hemispheres are alternately turned either toward or away from the Sun, depending on Earth's position in its orbit. The hemisphere turned toward the Sun receives more sunlight and is in summer, while the other hemisphere receives less sun and is in winter (see [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice")).

At the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox"), Earth's axis is perpendicular to the Sun rather than tilted toward or away, meaning that day and night are both about 12 hours long across the whole of Earth.

Near the equator, this means the variation in the strength of solar radiation is different relative to the time of year than it is at higher latitudes: maximum solar radiation is received during the equinoxes, when a place at the equator is under the [subsolar point](/wiki/Subsolar_point "Subsolar point") at high noon, and the intermediate seasons of spring and autumn occur at higher latitudes; and the minimum occurs during *both* solstices, when either pole is tilted towards or away from the sun, resulting in either summer or winter in both hemispheres. This also results in a corresponding movement of the equator away from the subsolar point, which is then situated over or near the relevant [tropic](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") [circle](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn"). Nevertheless, temperatures are high year-round due to the Earth's [axial tilt](/wiki/Axial_tilt "Axial tilt") of 23.5° not being enough to create a low minimum midday [declination](/wiki/Position_of_the_Sun#Declination_of_the_Sun_as_seen_from_Earth "Position of the Sun") to sufficiently weaken the Sun's rays even during the solstices. High year-round temperatures extend to about 25° north or south of the equator, although the moderate seasonal temperature difference is defined by the opposing solstices (as it is at higher latitudes) near the poleward limits of this range.

Near the equator, there is little temperature change throughout the year, though there may be dramatic differences in rainfall and humidity. The terms summer, autumn, winter and spring do not generally apply. Lowlands around the equator generally have a [tropical rainforest climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate"), also known as an equatorial climate, though cold ocean currents cause some regions to have [tropical monsoon climates](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") with a [dry season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season") in the middle of the year, and the [Somali Current](/wiki/Somali_Current "Somali Current") generated by the [Asian monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon_of_South_Asia "Monsoon of South Asia") due to continental heating via the high [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau") causes [Greater Somalia](/wiki/Greater_Somalia "Greater Somalia") to have an arid climate despite its equatorial location.

Average annual temperatures in equatorial lowlands are around 31 °C (88 °F) during the afternoon and 23 °C (73 °F) around sunrise. Rainfall is very high away from cold ocean current upwelling zones, from 2,500 to 3,500 mm (100 to 140 in) per year. There are about 200 rainy days per year and average annual sunshine hours are around 2,000. Despite high year-round sea level temperatures, some higher altitudes such as the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") and [Mount Kilimanjaro](/wiki/Mount_Kilimanjaro "Mount Kilimanjaro") have glaciers. The highest point on the equator is at the elevation of 4,690 metres (15,387 ft), at [0°0′0″N 77°59′31″W﻿ / ﻿0.00000°N 77.99194°W﻿ / 0.00000; -77.99194﻿ (highest point on the equator)](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Equator&params=0_0_0_N_77_59_31_W_type:landmark_region:EC&title=highest+point+on+the+equator), found on the southern slopes of [Volcán Cayambe](/wiki/Cayambe_(volcano) "Cayambe (volcano)") [summit 5,790 metres (18,996 ft)] in [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"). This is slightly above the [snow line](/wiki/Snow_line "Snow line") and is the only place on the equator where snow lies on the ground. At the equator, the snow line is around 1,000 metres (3,300 ft) *lower* than on [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest") and as much as 2,000 metres (6,600 ft) lower than the highest snow line in the world, near the [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn") on [Llullaillaco](/wiki/Llullaillaco "Llullaillaco").

## Line-crossing ceremonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equator&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Line-crossing ceremonies")]

Main article: [Line-crossing ceremony](/wiki/Line-crossing_ceremony "Line-crossing ceremony")

There is a widespread maritime tradition of holding ceremonies to mark a sailor's first crossing of the equator. In the past, these ceremonies have been notorious for their brutality, especially in naval practice.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Milder line-crossing ceremonies, typically featuring [King Neptune](/wiki/Neptune_(mythology) "Neptune (mythology)"), are also held for passengers' entertainment on some civilian ocean liners and cruise ships.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

"Equatorial launch sites offered certain advantages over facilities within the continental United States. A launching due east from a site on the equator could take advantage of the earth's maximum rotational velocity (460 m/s (1,510 ft/s)) to achieve orbital speed. The more frequent overhead passage of the orbiting vehicle above an equatorial base would facilitate tracking and communications. Most important, an equatorial launch site would avoid the costly dogleg technique, a prerequisite for placing rockets into equatorial orbit from sites such as Cape Canaveral, Florida (28 degrees north latitude). The necessary correction in the space vehicle's trajectory could be very expensive - engineers estimated that doglegging a Saturn vehicle into a low-altitude equatorial orbit from Cape Canaveral used enough extra propellant to reduce the payload by as much as 80%. In higher orbits, the penalty was less severe but still involved at least a 20% loss of payload."
